summary: I was born in Colfax, Wisconsin, on December 26, 1916. It's about fifty miles south and west of Rice Lake. I lived there until we moved to Rice Lake in 1924, so it was my birthplace and the start of my early life.
cues: fifty miles south and west of Rice Lake

summary: My family moved to Rice Lake, Wisconsin, in 1924 when I was about eight years old. We lived in town from November 1924 until April 1925, then moved out to a farm nearby. I lived there through the Great Depression and graduated high school in 1936 while living in that area.

During the Great Depression, my family was farming from 1925 until 1936. It was a tough time, but we managed to get by on the farm near Rice Lake. I remember doing various jobs like driving trucks and cutting wood after I graduated high school in 1936.

I joined the National Guard on October 6, 1940, and was inducted into federal service on October 15, 1940. Before that, I had some monthly training, but this was my first full-time service. The Guard was my entry into military life just before the U.S. entered World War II.

Our first military training camp was Camp Beauregard in Louisiana. I remember arriving there in the rain and helping cook bacon and eggs that ended up soaked with water. We had to set up our own tents and trained there before Pearl Harbor, using older equipment like 30 caliber machine guns.

The attack on Pearl Harbor happened while we were at Camp Livingston. It changed our training from peacetime to wartime, though we were already training seriously. After Pearl Harbor, the war production ramped up and our service became more urgent.

I was assigned a Browning Automatic Rifle when we got to New Guinea, and I learned how to take it apart and put it back together. I practiced shooting it at a thousand-yard range. It was a key weapon for me during combat in the South Pacific.

In 1939, I was visiting an uncle in Menominee when Germany invaded Poland. At that time, I didn't think much about the possibility of the U.S. entering the war. It was a distant event then, before the conflict reached us directly.

I recall the invasion of Poland by Germany in 1939 while I was in Menominee visiting family. It was a significant event that marked the start of the war in Europe, but I didn't think much about the U.S. getting involved at that time.

We moved to Camp Livingston, Louisiana, before the attack on Pearl Harbor. We were initially told we'd stay there for one year, but that changed indefinitely after the war started. The camp was part of our early wartime training.

I trained in Louisiana at Camp Beauregard and Camp Livingston. The weather was often rainy, and there was no jungle training since the terrain was different. We didn't get into towns much because our pay was low, mostly spending time on post.

After leaving Camp Livingston, we went to Fort Devens, Massachusetts, for about five or six weeks. It was a brief stop before we were sent to Fort Ord in California. Fort Devens was part of our transition to overseas deployment.

In April 1942, we arrived at Fort Ord, California, but only stayed about four or five days before heading overseas. A sergeant joked that infantry wouldn't be there long, and that proved true as we shipped out quickly. Fort Ord was our last stop before the South Pacific.

We traveled across the South Pacific Ocean aboard the USS Monterey, leaving San Francisco on April 22, 1942, and arriving in Australia on May 14. The trip was long, and many soldiers got seasick, though I did not. The South Pacific was the theater where I saw combat.

I sailed on the USS Monterey from San Francisco to Australia in April 1942. It was a large ship carrying freight and troops, but not heavily armed, so we were vulnerable if we met enemy battleships. The journey took about three weeks to reach Adelaide.

Many of the soldiers on the USS Monterey were from Michigan or Wisconsin, like me. We weren't used to being on the water, so seasickness was common among the group. Michigan was part of the regional background of many troops in my unit.

We landed in Adelaide, Australia, in May 1942. The Australians were very friendly, and the country was nice. We trained at Camp Woodside near Adelaide and had some military instruction including judo. I only got into Adelaide a couple of times during that period.

Adelaide was our first stop in Australia, where we landed after the long trip. I visited the city a couple of times, including once for a parade. The taxis were driven by charcoal burners, which was an interesting local detail I remember.

At Camp Woodside near Adelaide, we continued our military training. It was about thirty miles from Adelaide and we experienced a lot of rain and wet weather. We also received some judo instruction as part of our training there.

During our training at Camp Woodside in Australia, we received some instruction in judo. It wasn't extensive, but it was part of our military preparation. It was interesting to learn some hand-to-hand combat techniques while stationed there.

We went to Camp Table near Brisbane before heading to New Guinea. I was on the docks there and thought the convoy was lost because it was all woods around. Brisbane was a staging area for our further deployment into the jungle combat zones.

I was part of the 32nd Infantry Division during World War II. We fought in New Guinea, and I was assigned a Browning Automatic Rifle man for every other squad. The division saw its first combat in November 1942, where I was wounded.

We flew into Port Moresby in New Guinea, landing about seven miles from the airstrip. That night, the Japanese bombed the Air Corps nearby, but we were far enough away to avoid direct hits. Port Moresby was a key base for our operations in the jungle.

General Douglas MacArthur came out to our camp before we left for New Guinea. We prepared quickly and left within about two days after his visit. Seeing MacArthur was a memorable moment as he was a prominent figure in the Pacific campaign.

We faced the Imperial Japanese Army in New Guinea starting in November 1942. They bombed supply luggers and tried to cut through our lines, but their intelligence underestimated our troop numbers. The fighting was intense and dangerous in the jungle terrain.

After being wounded in New Guinea, I was sent to the 13th Station Hospital in Townsville, Australia. It was the first place I received emergency treatment after evacuation from the combat zone. Townsville was an important medical hub for wounded soldiers.

The 13th Station Hospital in Townsville was where I was treated after being wounded in New Guinea. They provided emergency care before I was transferred to other hospitals in Australia. It was a critical stop in my recovery process.
I was moved to the 105th General Hospital in Australia after Townsville. The hospital might have been an agricultural school before conversion. There I had surgery to treat my wounds from combat.

After the 105th, I was transported to the 118th General Hospital in Sydney, Australia. We occupied the entire fifth floor of Prince Albert's Hospital there. It was a longer stay where they continued my treatment before deciding to send me home.

Prince Albert's Hospital in Sydney was where the 118th General Hospital was located. I stayed on the fifth floor there during my recovery from wounds sustained in New Guinea. It was a significant part of my medical care overseas.

After returning to the United States, I was admitted to O'Reilly General Hospital in Springfield, Missouri. I stayed there until I reached the maximum hospitalization period and was discharged on August 24, 1943. It was the final stage of my recovery before returning home.

Springfield, Missouri, was where O'Reilly General Hospital is located, where I completed my recovery after being wounded in the Pacific. I was discharged there in August 1943 after months of treatment. Springfield marked the end of my military hospitalization.

While wounded in New Guinea, I was first treated by a medic from Indiana. He was one of the first to attend to me before I was evacuated to hospitals in Australia. Indiana was represented in the medical personnel who helped me on the battlefield.
